structured_choices.data_source: Historical transportation logs from a large urban delivery service covering 24 months. Raw data includes: timestamped delivery records (pickup time, delivery time, route distance, fuel consumed), demand features (order volume, geographic distribution, time of day), operational disturbances (weather events, traffic incidents, vehicle breakdowns), and unstructured customer reviews (processed for sentiment and service quality signals).
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome: next-day route efficiency, defined as delivery_time_minutes / baseline_expected_time_minutes, where baseline_expected_time is computed from historical averages stratified by route segment, time-of-week, and season. Secondary outcome: fuel_consumed_liters per delivery. Tertiary outcome: on-time delivery rate (binary: delivered within promised window). Labels are deterministic; no annotation required.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by season. Months 1–12 (Year 1) form the training set. Months 13–18 (first half of Year 2) form the validation set for model selection and hyperparameter tuning. Months 19–24 (second half of Year 2) form the held-out test set. This temporal ordering prevents information leakage (the RL model cannot access future demand or outcomes) and reflects the deployment reality (predict tomorrow's efficiency using today's and historical data). Stratification by season (summer/winter/spring/fall) ensures each split contains representative demand patterns; stratification checks confirm seasonal distribution is balanced across splits within ±5%.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are fit on the training set only (Months 1–12). Fitted transformers and statistics are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Unstructured review data (customer satisfaction signals) is processed using a pre-trained sentiment model (not retrained on this dataset) to avoid label leakage. No future information (Month N+1 onwards) is used to construct features for Month N predictions.
structured_choices.features: Input features for next-day routing: (1) Historical demand features: order volume (lag-1 to lag-7 days, stratified by geography), time-of-week and seasonal indicators, customer density heatmaps; (2) Operational features: available vehicle count, historical fuel efficiency by vehicle type, traffic patterns from past 30 days; (3) Contextual features: weather forecast for next day (temperature, precipitation), known events (holidays, road closures); (4) Quality signal: rolling 7-day average of customer on-time delivery rate and sentiment score from reviews. All features are lagged appropriately to represent information available at the time of routing decision (midnight for next-day routes).
structured_choices.model: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) or Deep Q-Network (DQN) trained to maximize a composite reward function: R = α·(−normalized_delivery_time) + β·(−normalized_fuel) + γ·(on_time_bonus) − δ·(service_degradation_penalty). The RL environment simulates daily routing decisions; the agent learns a policy mapping (demand state, vehicle fleet state, constraints) → route assignments. The model is trained on the training set using an episodic framework where each episode represents one day of routing decisions across all active routes. Training uses 50 epochs with experience replay and target network updates.
structured_choices.baseline: Traditional heuristics-based approach: nearest-neighbor greedy routing with periodic local optimization (2-opt swaps). This baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: heuristic parameters (e.g., swap iteration limit, demand forecast method) are tuned on the validation set (Months 13–18) using the same primary and secondary metrics as the DRL model. Both models use identical input features and are evaluated on identical test data (Months 19–24). The heuristic baseline is fair because it represents the current operational standard and uses only information available at decision time.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean route efficiency ratio (delivery_time / baseline_expected_time) on the test set, averaged across all routes and days. Target: achieve <0.95 ratio (5% improvement over expected). Justification: stakeholders prioritize delivery time reduction as the operational bottleneck; this metric directly measures whether the RL model reduces actual delivery time relative to historical norms, accounting for seasonal and demand-driven variability. A ratio <1.0 indicates faster delivery; stratified reporting by season and route type ensures no seasonal gaming.
secondary_metrics: Mean fuel consumption per delivery (liters), test set average; improvement target: <5% vs. baseline
On-time delivery rate (% of deliveries within promised window), test set; maintain ≥95%
Delivery capacity (orders completed per vehicle per day), test set average; target: ≥5% increase
Service quality proxy: rolling customer satisfaction (sentiment + on-time rate), test set; maintain ≥4.0/5.0
Policy stability: variance in efficiency ratio across consecutive days; should not exceed ±0.08
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Months 13–18) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. For the DRL agent: grid search over learning rates (1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3), reward weights (α, β, γ, δ combinations), and network architectures (2–4 hidden layers). For the baseline heuristic: tuning of swap iteration limits and forecast aggregation windows. The model achieving the best primary metric (efficiency ratio) on the validation set is selected for test evaluation. No test set data is examined during tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The held-out test set (Months 19–24) is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to generate final performance estimates. After model selection on validation data, both the DRL agent and baseline heuristic are run forward on the test set in a fixed, non-adaptive manner: no retraining, no parameter updates. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap over days) and stratified by season, route type, and demand quartile to detect any distributional mismatch.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Seasonal confounds: (1) Stratification ensures each split (train/val/test) contains proportional seasonal representation; (2) Primary metric (efficiency ratio) is normalized to historical expectations, so seasonal demand shifts do not inflate/deflate raw time; (3) Results are reported stratified by season to detect if gains are driven by seasonality rather than model quality. Operational disturbances (weather, traffic, breakdowns): (1) These are included as features for both DRL and baseline, ensuring fair comparison; (2) Test set spans 6 months (Months 19–24) to capture natural variability in disturbances; (3) Sensitivity analysis compares performance on high-disturbance vs. low-disturbance days. Unintended consequences (reduced service quality): The reward function explicitly includes a service_degradation_penalty term and on_time_bonus to discourage trading delivery time for reliability. Secondary metrics (on-time rate, customer satisfaction) are monitored as safety checks.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Deep RL vs. Heuristics for Next-Day Logistics Routing

### 1. Data Strategy and Preparation

We would use historical transportation logs from the delivery service spanning 24 months. The dataset includes timestamped delivery records (pickup/delivery times, route distances, fuel consumption), demand signals (order volume and geographic distribution by time of day), operational disturbances (weather, traffic, vehicle breakdowns), and customer reviews (for sentiment and service quality signals). All raw data is collected from the production environment, ensuring deployment alignment.

Labels are deterministically computed: route efficiency ratio = actual_delivery_time / baseline_expected_time, where the baseline is the historical median delivery time for that route segment, stratified by day-of-week, season, and demand level. This approach avoids annotation noise and accounts for inherent seasonal variability without requiring manual labeling.

Known data quality issues include sporadic missing fuel records (imputed using vehicle type and segment averages, fit on training data only) and occasional timestamp inconsistencies (resolved using trip distance validation). Unstructured review data is processed through a pre-trained sentiment model to extract customer satisfaction signals without retraining on this task.

### 2. Temporal Split with Seasonal Stratification

The 24-month dataset is divided chronologically to prevent information leakage:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (Year 1)
- **Validation set:** Months 13–18 (Year 2, first half)
- **Test set:** Months 19–24 (Year 2, second half)

This temporal ordering reflects real deployment: the RL agent learns from historical patterns and predicts tomorrow's efficiency using information available today. Critically, the agent cannot access future demand or outcomes.

To address seasonal confounding, each split is stratified to contain proportional representation of summer, winter, spring, and fall demand patterns. We verify stratification: each season's order volume and efficiency baseline should occupy 20–30% of each split (within ±5% tolerance). This ensures that performance gains are not driven by seasonal shifts but reflect genuine model improvements.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Input features are constructed to represent the state available at routing decision time (midnight for next-day routes):

- **Demand features:** Order volume (lags 1–7 days, aggregated by geography); time-of-week indicators (one-hot encoded); seasonal indicators; customer density heatmaps by delivery zone.
- **Operational features:** Available vehicle count by type; historical fuel efficiency by vehicle type; traffic patterns (average speed by segment, last 30 days).
- **Contextual features:** Next-day weather forecast (temperature, precipitation); known events (holidays, road closures).
- **Quality signal:** Rolling 7-day average on-time delivery rate and customer sentiment score.

All preprocessing (scaling, aggregation statistics) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents train-validation-test leakage. No future information is used to construct features for the current day.

### 4. Model and Baseline Design

The primary model is a Deep Reinforcement Learning agent using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The agent optimizes a composite reward function:

**R = α·(−normalized_delivery_time) + β·(−normalized_fuel) + γ·(on_time_bonus) − δ·(service_degradation_penalty)**

The RL environment simulates daily routing: the state includes current demand and vehicle availability; the action is a route assignment for each order; the reward reflects delivery time, fuel, and service quality. The agent is trained on 50 epochs with experience replay and target network stabilization.

The baseline is a traditional nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt local optimization. Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: heuristic parameters (swap iteration limit, forecast method) are grid-searched on the validation set using the same metrics as the DRL model. Both systems use identical input features and are evaluated on identical test data, ensuring fair comparison. The heuristic baseline represents the current operational standard and is a realistic performance ceiling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean route efficiency ratio on the test set (Months 19–24):

**Primary = mean(delivery_time / baseline_expected_time), target < 0.95**

Justification: Stakeholders prioritize delivery time reduction. This ratio is normalized to historical expectations, so it measures genuine improvement independent of seasonal demand fluctuations. A ratio <1.0 means faster delivery than historical norms; <0.95 represents a 5% improvement.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Mean fuel consumption per delivery (target: <5% improvement vs. baseline)
- On-time delivery rate (target: maintain ≥95%)
- Daily delivery capacity per vehicle (target: ≥5% increase)
- Customer satisfaction proxy: rolling sentiment + on-time rate (target: maintain ≥4.0/5.0)
- Policy stability: day-to-day variance in efficiency ratio (target: ±0.08)

Secondary metrics guard against unintended consequences (e.g., faster delivery at the cost of reliability) and ensure the reward function is properly aligned.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Strategy

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set (Months 13–18) only. For the DRL agent, we perform grid search over learning rates (1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3), reward weight combinations (α, β, γ, δ), and network architectures (2–4 hidden layers, 128–512 units). For the baseline heuristic, we tune swap iteration limits and forecast aggregation windows. The model achieving the best primary metric on validation data is selected.

The test set (Months 19–24) is held completely untouched during tuning and is used exactly once at the end to compute final performance estimates. Both models are run forward on test data in fixed, non-adaptive mode: no retraining, no updates. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap over days) and stratified by season, route type, and demand quartile to detect any distributional mismatch or cherry-picking.

### 7. Controlling Confounds and Establishing Scope

Seasonal confounds are addressed through stratified splitting and normalized metrics. The efficiency ratio metric accounts for seasonal demand shifts; results are reported by season to confirm gains are not seasonal artifacts.

Operational disturbances (weather, traffic, breakdowns) are included as features for both models, ensuring fair comparison. The 6-month test window captures natural variability in disturbances; sensitivity analysis compares performance on high-disturbance vs. low-disturbance days.

Unintended consequences (service quality degradation) are controlled by explicitly including service quality terms in the reward function and monitoring secondary metrics as safety gates.

**Scope:** This experiment establishes whether a properly tuned DRL agent outperforms a well-tuned heuristic baseline in next-day routing efficiency on this urban delivery service over a 6-month test window. It does not establish generalization to other cities, delivery models, or operational constraints not represented in the historical data. It also does not address online learning or continuous retraining—both models are frozen after the validation phase.
